title: Harness Lab
subtitle: Workshop operating system pro práci s AI agenty
principles: Pište tak, aby se dalo pokračovat

phases.label: Úvod a naladění
phases.goal: Otevřít den jako společný start: proč harness engineering dává smysl právě teď, co dnes budeme dělat od prvního kroku až po závěr dne a jak poznáme, že práce stojí na nohách i bez improvizované záchrany.
phases.roomSummary: Místnost má hned cítit, že tohle není promptovací hackathon. Je to den, ve kterém se budeme učit řídit agenty, stavět s nimi v reálném repu a odpoledne ověřit, co z práce přežije bez nás.
facilitatorPrompts: Dnes nezačínáme ukázkou nástroje ani soutěž v promptování. Začínáme den, ve kterém budeme řídit práci s coding agenty tak, aby po nás zůstalo něco použitelného.
Budete se učit, stavět, předávat i přebírat. Ten oblouk dne je záměr, ne logistika.
Neučíme se lépe promptovat. Učíme se postavit repo a workflow, ve kterém agent i cizí tým dokážou bezpečně pokračovat.
Co zůstane jen v hovoru u stolu, odpoledne nevydrží.
watchFors: Úvod sklouzne do pravidel dřív, než nabídne ambici a smysl dne.
Místnost to čte jako promptovací soutěž nebo závod ve funkcích.
Facilitátor příliš brzy vysvětlí continuation mechaniku místo toho, aby jen nastavil laťku pro pokračování.
checkpointQuestions: Kde by to našel další tým bez vás?
Co je tady skutečně ověřené?
Co si má místnost odnést do první build fáze?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Spustit den jako společný start pro místnost, ne jako provozní souhrn.

show: Promítni oblouk úvodu od dnešního slibu přes analogii a aktivaci až po první pracovní kontrakt.
Participant plochu ukaž až krátce na závěr openingu nebo v navazujícím přechodu.
do: Udělej rychlé rozdělení místnosti podle zkušenosti s AI agenty a nech zaznít dva krátké hlasy.
Drž úvod svižný: každý krok má změnit stav místnosti, ne vysvětlit celý workshop.
watch: Opening se nesmí proměnit v interní operating memo.
Lidé mají cítit ambici dne dřív, než dostanou první pravidlo.
fallback: Když čas klouže, nech framing, analogii a první kontrakt. Aktivaci zkrať na show of hands.
Když energie padá, vrať se k větě, že odpoledne prověří, co po nás zůstane bez záchrany.

scenes.label: Framing
scenes.title: Today you learn to shape the work so anyone — or any agent — can carry it
scenes.body: That's the craft underneath all the agent hype. Not better prompts. Not faster typing. A repo, a workflow, a context that carries the next move without you standing over it. By tonight you'll have built one with your hands, handed it to another team, and seen what survives. Monday morning, you'll open your editor and know what to build differently.
facilitatorNotes: Open with ambition and the participant destination, not logistics.
The hero title is the day's spine — read it out loud, then pause.
Cut any reference to prompt theatre or contest framing.

blocks.title: Today you learn to shape the work so anyone — or any agent — can carry it
blocks.body: That's the craft underneath all the agent hype. Not better prompts. Not faster typing. A repo, a workflow, a context that carries the next move without you standing over it. By tonight you'll have built one with your hands, handed it to another team, and seen what survives. Monday morning, you'll open your editor and know what to build differently.

blocks.title: The main line for today
blocks.body: We are not learning to prompt better. We are learning to build a working system that carries the next move — whether the next reader is a teammate who joined today or a fresh agent you open on Monday.

blocks.title: What should change today
blocks.body: Monday morning you open a fresh agent in your own repo. That agent arrives without your memory. What you practice today is how you treat every session — not once, for every session.

scenes.label: Day arc
scenes.title: Learn. Build. Hand off. Continue.
scenes.body: One day, four moves. Each one sets up the next. By the time the day closes, you will have practiced each move end to end.
facilitatorNotes: Give each step one sentence. Keep the spine tight.
No rules yet — just the promise of the arc.

blocks.title: Learn. Build. Hand off. Continue.
blocks.body: This is not a prompting contest. It is a day where you will learn the craft, build something real, hand it to another team, and watch what continues without you.

items.title: Learn.
items.body: The talk and the demo name the craft and show you what it looks like in a real repo.

items.title: Build.
items.body: Your team picks a brief and builds a working slice — with AGENTS.md as a map, a plan, a tracer, and a next-move note.

items.title: Hand off.
items.body: After lunch, your repo is not yours anymore. Another team inherits it cold. You inherit theirs.

items.title: Continue.
items.body: You continue in the inherited repo — reading, capturing friction, shipping another slice. The afternoon tests what the morning encoded.

scenes.label: Day schedule
scenes.title: The shape of the day
scenes.body: These are guides, not deadlines. Lunch in particular is negotiable on the day.
facilitatorNotes: Place this before team formation so teams know what they're joining.
Read the afternoon split once — Build 2 splits around Intermezzo 2.

blocks.title: The shape of the day

blocks.title: Times are guides, not deadlines.

scenes.label: Experience line + team formation
scenes.title: Form the line. Count off. Claim your anchor.
scenes.body: Nine minutes. We form teams by experience line, then each team claims an anchor — a physical marker that the team, the repo, and the day's check-ins live at.
facilitatorNotes: Start by having everyone stand and form one line by experience with AI agents.
Count off live based on head count. Announce the target team size before counting.
Team names go into the admin dashboard live as they come in.
If the room stalls past four minutes, start assigning manually.

blocks.title: Form the line. Count off. Claim your anchor.
blocks.body: Nine minutes. This is how we form teams for the day — by experience line and count-off, the same mechanic we'll use again at rotation after lunch.

items.title: Form the line (3 min).
items.body: Everyone stands up. Form one line along the wall — most experienced with AI agents at the front, newest at the back. Argue with each other about where you stand. This is the map of the room.

items.title: Count off into teams (1 min).
items.body: Count off down the line based on the target team size. All the 1s form a team, all the 2s, and so on. Every team automatically spans the full experience spectrum.

items.title: Sit, introduce, name (4 min).
items.body: Go to a table together. Tell each other your name and one sentence about what brought you here. Pick a team name.

items.title: Claim your anchor.
items.body: Pick one of the anchors on the tables — a Lego brick, a numbered card, whatever's in front of you. The anchor stays at the table. Your team, your repo, and your check-ins live with it for the rest of the day.

items.title: We record it live.
items.body: Raise a hand with your team name. Facilitator types it into the dashboard; it appears on the participant board in front of you.

blocks.title: Why mix experience.
blocks.body: A team where everyone works with agents the same way will only build what they already know. A mixed team will argue, and the argument is where harness engineering actually happens.

scenes.label: Handoff to the talk
scenes.title: Your team is on the board. Look up.
scenes.body: Brief transition beat. Facilitator moves the room into the talk.
facilitatorNotes: 30-second beat. Room reset, then straight into the talk.
May be collapsed into a verbal transition if the team formation runs long.

blocks.title: Your team is on the board. Look up.
blocks.body: Next: the talk. We start by naming the craft underneath all the agent hype.

phases.label: The Craft Underneath
phases.goal: Proměnit energii z openingu v přesnou tezi: harness engineering je týmová infrastruktura pro práci s agenty a první build krok musí začít mapou, mantinely a ověřením, ne dalším promptem.
phases.roomSummary: Po talku má být jasné, že `AGENTS.md`, skills, runbooky a explicitní ověření nejsou doplněk. Jsou to pracovní rozhraní, která přenášejí záměr do dalšího kroku a snižují cenu předání i review.
facilitatorPrompts: Neučíme se lépe promptovat. Učíme se postavit repo a workflow, ve kterém agent i cizí tým dokážou bezpečně pokračovat.
Co není v repu, neexistuje.
Když agent dělá víc, vy musíte lépe ověřovat.
Po tomhle talku se vracíte k repu s konkrétním pracovním systémem, ne s pocitem, že potřebujete jen chytřejší prompt.
watchFors: Talk sklouzává k předvádění funkcí nástroje.
Týmy slyší hlavně tipy k promptování místo workflow disciplíny.
Chybí čistý most do první build fáze.
checkpointQuestions: Co dnes přesunete z hlavy nebo z chatu do repa?
Který signál v repu by pomohl cizímu týmu navázat?
Jaké explicitní ověření dnes bude vaší hranicí důvěry?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Udělat z harness engineeringu přesnou, zapamatovatelnou tezi a poslat místnost zpátky do repa s konkrétním prvním pracovním kontraktem.
say: Začni kontrastem připravenosti repa: stejný prompt, dvě repa. Nech místnost vidět rozdíl dřív, než ho pojmenuješ.
Po kontrastu se zeptej: 'Co se změnilo?' Nech promluvit dva hlasy.
Pojmenuj, co jsi viděl: agent v holém repu dělal uvěřitelná, ale špatná rozhodnutí, protože neměl mantinely. Tohle je task drift.
Kontext je páka, ne kosmetika. Práci udělalo repo, ne prompt.
show: Promítni nejdřív kontrast připravenosti repa, pak tezi, pak přechod do buildu.
Participant pohled ukaž jen jako potvrzení, že room, dashboard a repo drží stejný moment.
do: Spusť kontrast: ukaž nejdřív holé repo, pak repo s harnessem a stejným promptem.
Když kontrast dosedne, přejdi na tezi. Řekni ji jednou jasně.
Pošli místnost do build fáze 1 s jedním očekáváním: nejdřív mapa a ověření, teprve potom práci na funkcích.
watch: Talk nesmí sklouznout k tipům na promptování nebo přehlídce funkcí nástroje.
Místnost má po talku vědět, co má udělat v repu během příštích minut.
fallback: Když se demo vleče, použij připravené screenshoty. Kontrast je důležitější než živé generování.
Když je pozornost nízko, nech hlavní větu, team-lead analogii a jeden konkrétní build expectation.

scenes.label: Something changed
scenes.title: While we argued about prompts, something changed
scenes.body: Six months ago the conversation was still about prompts. Today it's about what the agent reads when it opens your repo. The teams that figured that out are shipping in ways that looked impossible last year. Let me show you what changed — then we'll spend the rest of the day practicing it.
facilitatorNotes: Scene 2.1 is the densest scene of the day. Deliberate pause after each named voice.
If the room is fatigued, the Gartner/Forrester references are the first thing to cut.

blocks.title: While we argued about prompts, something changed
blocks.body: Six months ago the conversation was still about prompts. Today it's about what the agent reads when it opens your repo. The teams that figured that out are shipping in ways that looked impossible last year. Let me show you what changed — then we'll spend the rest of the day practicing it.

items.title: Ryan Lopopolo at OpenAI, February.
items.body: Led a team at OpenAI Frontier & Symphony that shipped a production beta with one million lines of code in five months. Zero lines written by hand. One billion tokens a day. Zero percent human review. He published the playbook. Its first principle: "Humans steer. Agents execute."

items.title: Birgitta Böckeler at Thoughtworks, ten days ago.
items.body: Published on Martin Fowler's site the cleanest definition of the craft underneath all of this. She called it harness engineering and defined it as the discipline of the entire environment an agent works inside — guides that steer it before it acts, sensors that catch it after.

items.title: Charlie Guo at OpenAI, also February.
items.body: Developer experience engineer, watching the shift from inside. His line: "I'm moving away from chatting with AIs and moving towards managing them." That is what happens to your role when harness engineering works.

items.title: Mitchell Hashimoto at HashiCorp, earlier this year.
items.body: His test for whether you're actually practicing the craft: "Anytime you find an agent makes   mistake, you take the time to engineer   solution such that the agent never makes that mistake again." That is a one-sentence definition of a sensor.

items.title: Stripe's Minions project, quietly shipping.
items.body: Over a thousand PRs a week landing for review with no human between the task being posted and the pull request arriving. Not a demo. Production.

blocks.title: Nobody actually knows what December looks like.
blocks.body: The analyst firms are already projecting 2028 numbers. Honestly? Nobody knows what this looks like at the end of 2026. Simon Willison — a working developer, one of the most level-headed voices in this space — opened his January predictions post by saying "I'm certain that things will change significantly, but unclear as to what those changes will be." In the same post, he mentioned that since Opus 4.5 and GPT-5.2 shipped late last year, the amount of code he writes by hand had dropped to a single digit percentage of his output. The weather is moving faster than anyone predicted. The craft we're practicing today is the hedge against a year none of us can see.

scenes.label: It got a name
scenes.title: Last week, it got a name
scenes.body: Ten days ago, Birgitta Böckeler published this equation on Martin Fowler's site: Agent = Model + Harness. It's the cleanest version I've seen. The model has the power. The harness is what turns that power into useful work instead of drift.
facilitatorNotes: Hold the engine/chassis analogy as a one-time image. If you extend it later, be consistent.

blocks.title: Last week, it got a name
blocks.body: Ten days ago, Birgitta Böckeler published this equation on Martin Fowler's site: Agent = Model + Harness. The model has the power. The harness is what turns that power into useful work instead of drift.
A good harness doesn't aim to eliminate human input. It directs human input to where it matters most.

Think of a raw engine. A powerful one — four hundred horsepower, capable of more than most drivers can handle. Bolt it to a shopping cart and you have a very expensive way to destroy a parking lot. Bolt it to a well-designed chassis — frame, suspension, brakes, transmission, proper tires — and suddenly the car becomes drivable by someone who isn't a professional. The chassis didn't make the engine less powerful. It made the power accessible, predictable, survivable. That's the model. That's the harness.

blocks.title: The gap between models is narrowing. The gap between harnesses is widening.
blocks.body: Two teams using the same Claude or the same GPT can ship wildly different outcomes — not because of the model, but because of the environment the model runs in. That environment has a name now. Today we learn to build it.

scenes.label: The four pillars
scenes.title: How you actually build one
scenes.body: Fowler splits it into guides and sensors. OpenAI splits it into context, constraints, and feedback. Guo frames it as managing instead of chatting. I'm going to give you the four pillars I've watched actually work — across all of those framings. These are the four you'll practice before lunch.
facilitatorNotes: Densest scene in the talk. Target 6–8 minutes; pre-cut version collapses pillars 2 and 3 into one.

blocks.title: How you actually build one
blocks.body: Four pillars. Each one is a move you'll practice today.

items.title: Context as infrastructure.
items.body: The repo is the agent's memory. AGENTS.md, skills, runbooks — team infrastructure, not documentation. If it's not in a file, it doesn't exist. That standard has a name — AGENTS.md — and the Linux Foundation now stewards it.

items.title: Guides — steering before it acts.
items.body: Architectural rules, templates, constraints the agent literally cannot break. OpenAI's harness enforced layered architecture mechanically so drift was impossible, not just discouraged.

items.title: Sensors — catching after it acts.
items.body: Tracer bullets, end-to-end tests, automated reviews — feedback optimized for the agent to consume, and scoped holistically. When the agent does more, your verification has to prove the whole thing works, not that one function returned 4.

items.title: Managing, not chatting.
items.body: You stop being a pair-programmer and become a director with a crew. "I'm moving away from chatting with AIs and moving towards managing them." — Charlie Guo.

blocks.title: A team lead builds the system the team runs in.
blocks.body: They don't tell each developer what to do every thirty seconds. They build the rules, rituals, and feedback loops the team runs in. Today we practice that exact shift — for agents.

items.text: Guides that steer before the agent acts.
Sensors that catch after the agent acts.

scenes.label: The core line
scenes.title: Humans steer. Agents execute.
scenes.body: Four words from Ryan Lopopolo, the OpenAI engineer who led the team that shipped a million lines of production code in five months with zero lines written by hand. This is his foundational principle — the one he says everything else rests on. I want you to carry these four words back to Monday. Everything else you learn today is a way of making them real.
facilitatorNotes: This is the most memorable line in the whole workshop. Protect it.

blocks.title: Humans steer. Agents execute.
blocks.body: Four words from Ryan Lopopolo, OpenAI Frontier & Symphony. The team that shipped a million lines of production code in five months with zero lines written by hand. His foundational principle — everything else rests on this.
Give Codex a map, not a 1,000-page instruction manual.
Anytime you find an agent makes a mistake, you take the time to engineer a solution such that the agent never makes that mistake again.

blocks.title: Monday morning, you'll open your editor and work differently.
blocks.body: Not with a new tool. With a different role. You'll stop being the person the agent asks questions of, and start being the person who builds the room it walks into.

scenes.label: You've been sitting inside one
scenes.title: You've been sitting inside one all morning
scenes.body: The workshop skill. The dashboard. The participant board in front of you. The repo you're about to open. I built all of it with agents, using the discipline we just named. Not as a sales pitch — as proof. The craft is real. It works. Today we're practicing inside an example of it.
facilitatorNotes: Facilitator transition line: "Let me show you what   mean — here'  what   working agent session actually looks like when the harness is doing its job."

blocks.title: You've been sitting inside one all morning
blocks.body: The workshop skill, the dashboard, the participant board, the repo you're about to open — all of it was built with agents, using the discipline we just named. What you're about to see in the demo is a working instance, not a promise.

blocks.title: Same prompt. Two repos. Watch what happens.
blocks.body: I'm going to run the same simple prompt in two repos — one bare, one with a harness. Same words. Same model. Watch what changes.

items.title: Folder A — the bare repo.
items.body: Project brief, nothing else. No AGENTS.md. No constraints. No plan. Most repos on Monday morning look exactly like this.

items.title: Folder B — the same repo, with a harness.
items.body: Same brief. Plus AGENTS.md (Goal, Context, Constraints, Done When). Plus a short plan. Plus the workshop skill installed.

blocks.title: That was task drift.
blocks.body: The agent started without constraints and made plausible but wrong decisions. Without a harness, it filled in the blanks with its own assumptions. This is what happens in every repo without AGENTS.md. It has a name: task drift. Once you can name it, you can build for it.

blocks.title: The prompt didn't change. The repo did.
blocks.body: Same words, same model, different harness, different outcome. That's the whole day in thirty seconds.

scenes.label: The full flow
scenes.title: Now watch it hold together
scenes.body: I'm staying in Folder B. I'm going to run the actual workflow — the one you'll be running in twenty minutes. Four beats. Watch how each one feeds the next.
facilitatorNotes: Keep the beats crisp. One pass, four moves.

sourceRefs.label: Demo script: Let me show you

blocks.title: Now watch it hold together
blocks.body: Four beats. Each one feeds the next. This is the workflow you'll run in your own Folder B in twenty minutes.

items.title: AGENTS.md — the map.
items.body: Goal, Context, Constraints, Done When. Four headings. The agent reads this first, every session. This is the team's memory.

items.title: /plan — the next safe step.
items.body: The agent breaks the work into bounded steps. You see the plan before anything is written.

items.title: Small slice.
items.body: The agent implements one bounded piece. Not the whole feature. Small enough to verify.

items.title: /review — change control inside the flow.
items.body: Review is not a panic brake at the end. It's how the agent and the team agree on what just shipped.

blocks.title: Review is not an emergency brake.
blocks.body: It's part of the workflow. Every beat feeds the next. When something goes wrong — and things go wrong — you catch it one beat away, not ten.

blocks.title: Your toolkit, same discipline
blocks.body: One command to install. One tool that ties the morning together. Watch me install it here — you'll do the same thing in twenty minutes.

items.title: One command to install.
items.body: In your team's repo. That's it.

items.title: Your brief, on demand.
items.body: The skill knows your team, your project, your phase. Ask it, it hands you what you need.

items.title: Reference material when you're stuck.
items.body: Patterns, examples, checks. Not a tutorial — a reference you pull at the moment of need.

items.title: A place to record what you learn.
items.body: Every time you find a mistake worth fixing for next time, it lives here.

blocks.title: Hashimoto's rule, operationalized.
blocks.body: Anytime you find an agent makes a mistake, you take the time to engineer a solution such that the agent never makes that mistake again. This skill is where you do that — in a file, in the repo, for the next person. Or the next you.

scenes.label: Your first ten minutes
scenes.title: Your first ten minutes
scenes.body: In ten minutes I want every team at the point where the agent is actually helping you draft your plan. Here's how you get there — four moves. Don't skip any of them.
facilitatorNotes: Facilitator transition to Build 1: "Clock starts now. Build Phase 1 is open."

sourceRefs.label: Demo script: Let me show you

blocks.title: Your first ten minutes
blocks.body: Four moves. Don't skip any of them. In ten minutes I want every team at the point where the agent is actually helping you draft your plan.

items.title: Open your team's repo.
items.body: Create from template if you need to. One person opens it for everyone.

items.title: Install the workshop skill.
items.body: Same command I just ran. If it breaks, raise a hand — someone will come to you.

items.title: Agree on what you're building.
items.body: Ask the skill for the list of prepared briefs, or propose your own. Five minutes to decide, not fifty.

items.title: Start your AGENTS.md with the agent.
items.body: Open a session and draft three things together: what done looks like, what's not in scope, what you'll verify first. The agent is your first collaborator. Let it ask you questions.

blocks.title: Align with your team, then align with the agent.
blocks.body: Your team has to know what it wants before you can ask the agent for it. Five minutes of team alignment saves an hour of drift.

blocks.title: Your ambition is welcome — once the harness holds.
blocks.body: These briefs describe a shape. How far you take it is up to your team. If you want to plug your standup bot into your actual Slack, do it. If you want your metrics dashboard on your real data, do it. The harness comes first — but once the harness holds, go as far as your team can reach. Monday is the point.

Vracejte týmům artefakty, ze kterých se dá pracovat, ne celý facilitátorský backstage.
watchFors: Tým generuje text dál, ale nic neověřuje.
AGENTS.md bobtná do skladiště všeho.
Není jasné, co je hotové, co rozpracované a co jen hypotéza.

Přibývá kontext v repu, nebo zůstává v hlavách a v chatu?

blocks.title: The next 65 minutes

items.title: 10:30–10:40 · Set up.
items.body: Repo open. Skill installed. Team aligned on what you're building. First AGENTS.md draft started with the agent.

items.title: 10:40–11:00 · Plan and scaffold.
items.body: AGENTS.md as a map — goal, context, done-when, pointers. Not a warehouse. A short plan for the first slice. Before you write the code, sketch your first tracer — the thin end-to-end path that will prove the system holds together.

items.title: 11:00–11:20 · Ship the first slice.
items.body: One bounded piece of work. One tracer that runs. Commit it.

items.title: 11:20–11:30 · Iterate or extend.
items.body: If the tracer holds, push further. If it doesn't, fix the verification and ship again.

items.title: 11:30–11:35 · Final check.
items.body: Run your tracer yourself. Read your repo with fresh eyes. Does it hold everything someone else would need to continue?

items.text: AGENTS.md — a map. Goal, context, done-when, pointers. Not a warehouse.
A plan. Short, followable. Wherever it makes sense.
A working slice with its tracer. One bounded piece of code plus the end-to-end path that proves it runs.
A next-move note. What to do next, in the repo, not in your heads.

blocks.title: Holistic beats granular.
blocks.body: With agents, your verification needs to prove the whole thing works, not that one function returned 4. Start with a tracer — a thin end-to-end path you can run. Then add automation so the tracer runs on every change. Add review — automated or human — only where judgment is needed. Unit tests are fine, but they are not the point. The more the agent does, the more holistic your checks have to be.

scenes.label: Return to the proof
scenes.title: Return to the proof
scenes.body: More prompting won't unstick you. Step back. The answer is already in one of three places.
facilitatorNotes: Flip to this scene when a team is stuck, or project room-wide at a checkpoint moment.
Coach first, mentor second, teach only as a last resort.

blocks.title: Return to the proof
blocks.body: More prompting won't unstick you. Step back. The answer is already in one of three places.

items.title: State what you're trying to prove.
items.body: Out loud, at the table. What are you actually trying to do? How will you know you moved forward? If the team can't answer, that is the block.

items.title: Find the blind spot in the repo.
items.body: Is the problem in the README? AGENTS.md? The plan? The verification? The place the repo can't answer your question is the place to fix.

items.title: Add the smallest check.
items.body: A test. A tracer bullet. A one-line script that proves reality matches your mental model. Any executable signal beats more guessing.

blocks.title: If a facilitator hands you an answer, it didn't help.
blocks.body: Ask for a question, not an answer. If you leave the conversation with a spoken solution but no repo artifact, the problem will come back an hour later. Return the fix to the repo.

blocks.title: The one question to write against.
blocks.body: What surprised you about working with the agent this morning — and what does that make you want to try differently next? Three minutes. Don't talk yet.

scenes.label: Your team's check-in
scenes.title: Your team's check-in
scenes.body: Five minutes at your table. Compare what you wrote. Then one person writes the team's check-in into the team card on your participant board. Short, honest — two or three sentences is fine. It becomes part of your team's record.
facilitatorNotes: Team card append-only check-in log is load-bearing here. The entry persists across the day.

blocks.title: Your team's check-in
blocks.body: Compare what you wrote. One person types the team's check-in into the team card. Short, honest — two or three sentences.

items.title: Share what you wrote (3 min).
items.body: Go around the table. Read what you wrote out loud to each other. No debate, just listen.

items.title: Agree on the check-in (1 min).
items.body: What's actually true about your team right now? What's surprising? What do you want to try differently?

items.title: One person writes it (1 min).
items.body: On the participant board, in the team check-in field. It saves automatically and becomes part of your team's record.

blocks.title: If it's not recorded, it didn't happen.
blocks.body: Same rule as the repo. A check-in spoken at the table and lost is not a check-in. Type it. Two sentences is enough. It will be here for your future self and for whoever reads this project next.

scenes.label: The thread
scenes.title: The thread
scenes.body: Your check-ins are on screen. I'm going to read them out loud, one team at a time. Then I'll pull the thread that connects them — one principle point for the morning.
facilitatorNotes: The principle point is ad hoc, read from the room. Don't pre-can it.

blocks.title: The thread
blocks.body: Your check-ins are on screen. I read them out loud, one team at a time. Then I pull the thread that connects them — one principle point for the morning.

items.title: Read the check-ins on screen.
items.body: Facilitator scrolls through each team's recorded check-in and reads it aloud. No debate, just listening.

items.title: The thread.
items.body: Facilitator names the one thing that connects the check-ins — a pattern, a tension, a shared gap, a common win. Grounded in what is literally on screen, not in impressions.

items.title: One thing to carry.
items.body: What each person takes into the afternoon. Verbal, or written back into the team card — facilitator's call.

blocks.title: The principle comes from the screen, not from vibes.
blocks.body: Every team's check-in is on screen. The thread is whatever is actually there, not what the facilitator hopes is there.

scenes.label: Your repo is not yours anymore
scenes.title: Your repo is not yours anymore
scenes.body: For the rest of the day, you are working in the repo another team built this morning. Another team is working in yours. The team names — and the anchors that mark them — stay where they are. The people move. Everything you left in the repo at lunch is everything the new team has.
facilitatorNotes: The reveal beat. Keep it calm, direct.

blocks.title: Your repo is not yours anymore
blocks.body: For the rest of the day, you are working in the repo another team built this morning. Another team is working in yours. Everything you left in the repo at lunch is everything the new team has.

blocks.title: This isn't an ambush. It's the test.
blocks.body: The opening said you would hand off and inherit. Lunch was the gate. Whatever you left in the repo at noon is everything the new team has. Whatever is still in your heads — they will never hear it. This is what we practiced for.

scenes.label: Line up, count off, walk to the anchor
scenes.title: Line up, count off, walk to the anchor
scenes.body: I'm going to call a count-off — same shape as this morning. Line up at your old anchor, count off one through N, and remember your number. When I say go, everyone with the same number forms a new team at the anchor that matches. The repo, the history, and the check-in are already waiting.
facilitatorNotes: Physical movement. Enforce the silence rule gently but firmly.

blocks.title: Line up, count off, walk to the anchor
blocks.body: Same mechanic as this morning. Your number tells you which anchor to walk to.

items.title: Stand up at your old anchor.
items.body: Laptop, notebook, whatever you need — bring it with you.

items.title: Count off along your team.
items.body: One, two, three, four, five. Remember your number.

items.title: Walk to the anchor with your number.
items.body: All the 1s to Anchor 1, all the 2s to Anchor 2, and so on. The repo, the Intermezzo 1 check-in, and the team name are already there.

items.title: Introduce yourselves — properly (3–5 min).
items.body: Three things each: (1) your name and one thing you care about in software, (2) one thing you're bringing from your old team, (3) one thing you want to know about this new repo.

blocks.title: Your old repo is not yours anymore. Do not help.
blocks.body: You are leaving your old team's repo behind. The people arriving there have to read it cold. If they catch your eye, smile and point at the repo. The silence is the test — for you too.

scenes.label: Every fresh agent session is a rotation
scenes.title: Every fresh agent session is a rotation
scenes.body: A new agent has no memory of the previous session. A new teammate next month opens your repo the same way. The exercise you're about to do — reading a stranger's repo cold — is what every fresh agent session looks like. Monday morning, every morning, it's the same move. We're just practicing it out loud, with humans, at scale.
facilitatorNotes: This is where rotation stops being a gimmick and becomes daily practice.

blocks.eyebrow: Now you sit in the other team's work
blocks.title: Every fresh agent session is a rotation
blocks.body: Reading a stranger's repo cold is what every fresh agent session looks like. Monday morning, every morning, it's the same move.

items.title: Read (5 min).
items.body: README, AGENTS.md, the plan, the latest commits, the team's Intermezzo 1 check-in. No keyboard. No conversation with anyone from the original team.

items.title: Map (3 min).
items.body: Talk at your new table. What helps? What's missing? What's risky? What's the next safe move?

items.title: Diagnose on the team card (2 min).
items.body: Write your team's diagnosis of the inherited repo directly onto the team card — not as status, as a recorded artifact. It joins the Intermezzo 1 check-in as part of the repo's history. Include the new team composition (who's in the team now).

items.title: Then start.
items.body: Only after the diagnosis is written do you touch code.

blocks.title: If this is confusing, that's the point.
blocks.body: If the repo you just inherited is confusing to read, that isn't bad luck — it's what weak context feels like from the other side. Your job for the next ten minutes is to notice what's missing and write it down. Do not rescue the repo with conversation. The confusion is the signal.

Každá opakující se bolest je kandidát na lepší template, ověření nebo runbook.
watchFors: Tým jen opravuje chaos, ale neproměňuje ho v lepší návod pro další práci.
Slabý signál v repu se pořád zachraňuje hovorem.
Chybí explicitní nová verifikace po převzetí práce.

One new move: when the inherited repo trips you, capture the friction into the repo as you go. Eighty-five minutes of building, split by a twenty-minute pause in the middle. Build is the goal. Capturing friction is how you build without tripping on the same thing twice.

items.title: 13:45–13:55 · Quiet read.
items.body: Ten minutes, no keyboard. Read the repo, write your diagnosis and new team composition on the team card.

items.title: 13:55–14:00 · Capture the friction.
items.body: Five minutes. You just read a stranger's repo. What tripped you? What was missing? Write it into the repo — a clearer AGENTS.md section, a rule, a missing check. Five minutes of cleanup earns an hour of clean building.

items.title: 14:00–14:30 · First push — ship a slice.
items.body: Thirty minutes. One bounded piece, one tracer that runs, committed.

items.title: 14:30–14:50 · Mid-point pause (Intermezzo 2).
items.body: Twenty minutes. Step back with the room, check in, then back to your team. This is a pause, not an ending.

items.title: 14:50–15:20 · Second push — build it out.
items.body: Thirty minutes. Iterate, extend, push further. Fix what the first push did not. Apply whatever you surfaced in the intermezzo.

items.title: 15:20–15:30 · Final cleanup.
items.body: Ten minutes. Commit. Make your repo readable. Decide what you will show at Reveal.

items.text: AGENTS.md as a map. Goal, context, done-when, pointers. Not a warehouse.
A working slice with its tracer. One bounded piece of code, plus the end-to-end path that proves it runs.
A next-move note. What to do next, in the repo, not in your heads.

blocks.title: Watch out for the self-validation trap.
blocks.body: When the agent generates tests in the same task it generates code, those tests reflect the agent's interpretation of the spec — not the spec itself. Independent verification means: you wrote the done criteria, or a separate pass evaluates the output. This is a quiet trap. Most teams fall into it once. Name it when you see it, and split the work — one pass writes the check, a different pass writes the code.

scenes.label: Your team's check-in
scenes.title: Your team's check-in
scenes.body: Five minutes at your table. Compare what you wrote. Then one person writes the team's check-in on your team card. Short, honest — two or three sentences is fine. Another entry in the story the card is telling.
facilitatorNotes: This is not the final check-in. Another entry in the append-only log.

blocks.title: Your team's check-in
blocks.body: Compare what you wrote. Append to the team card. Two or three sentences.

items.title: Share what you wrote (3 min).
items.body: Go around the table. Read it out loud. No debate.

items.title: Agree on the check-in (1 min).
items.body: What does your team want to try in the second push?

items.title: One person writes it (1 min).
items.body: On the participant board, appended to the team card alongside the Intermezzo 1 check-in and the Build 2 first-beat diagnosis.

blocks.title: This is one of several check-ins.
blocks.body: The team card is accumulating a story. Intermezzo 1 check-in, rotation diagnosis, now this, and maybe more. Don't treat it like a final report. Treat it like a commit message — a small honest note about where your team is right now.

scenes.label: Back to the work
scenes.title: Back to the work
scenes.body: Your check-ins are on screen. I'll read them, pull the thread, and send you back to your team with one thing to try in the second push.
facilitatorNotes: Don't let Intermezzo 2 become a mini-Reveal. Keep it brisk.

blocks.title: Back to the work
blocks.body: I read your check-ins, pull the thread, and send you back with one thing to try in the second push.

items.title: Read the check-ins on screen.
items.body: Facilitator scrolls through each team's check-in and reads it aloud. Same rhythm as the first intermezzo.

items.title: The thread.
items.body: Facilitator names the shared pattern across the first push — what teams are discovering about the inherited repo, where the friction is concentrated. Grounded in what is literally on screen.

items.title: Back to work.
items.body: No extended reflection, no closing framing. Closing line: "Forty more minutes. Back to your teams."

blocks.title: This is a pause, not an ending.
blocks.body: Same rhythm as the first intermezzo — retrieve, share, move on. No carrying home yet. That is for later. Right now: what is working, what is not, and what you want to do in the next forty minutes.

phases.label: Build fáze 2 · druhý push
phases.goal: Aplikujte signál z mezipatra. Iterujte, rozšiřujte, tlačte dál. Commitněte a udělejte repo čitelné pro Reveal.
phases.roomSummary: Druhý push Build 2. Tým jedná podle toho, co zvedlo mezipatro, a připravuje repo pro Reveal.
facilitatorPrompts: Právě jste se zastavili. Teď jednejte podle signálu.
Commitněte, co drží. Udělejte repo čitelné. Rozhodněte, co ukážete na Reveal.
watchFors: Týmy místo uzavření slice přepíšou příliš mnoho.
Týmy vynechají final cleanup a dorazí na Reveal s repem, které nikdo nepřečte.
checkpointQuestions: Co vám řeklo mezipatro? Jednali jste podle toho?
Je repo teď čitelné pro cizince?
Co ukážete na Reveal?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Hold the tempo from the first push. The work is the same; the test is whether the team can act on the intermezzo signal.
say: Forty minutes. Back to the work.
Apply what you surfaced in the intermezzo.
At 15:30, commit and decide what you'll show.
show: Ambient timeline scene. Recovery pattern one flip away.
do: Walk the room. Watch for teams skipping the final cleanup.
watch: Teams over-extend instead of closing the slice cleanly.
Self-validation trap re-appearing in the second push.
fallback: If a team loses the thread, flip to the recovery scene and coach them back to the proof.

scenes.label: Clock resumes
scenes.title: Second push. Forty minutes. Back to the work.
scenes.body: Apply whatever you surfaced in the intermezzo. Iterate, extend, push further. At 15:30, commit, make your repo readable, decide what you'll show at Reveal.
facilitatorNotes: 30-second beat. Keep the tempo the room already knows from Build 1 and Build 2 first push.

blocks.title: Second push. Forty minutes. Back to the work.
blocks.body: Apply whatever you surfaced in the intermezzo. Iterate, extend, push further. At 15:30, commit and decide what you'll show at Reveal.

scenes.label: The second push
scenes.title: Forty minutes left. Make it count.
scenes.body: The work is the same discipline. The test is whether you can take the signal from the intermezzo and act on it. If you get stuck, the recovery pattern is one flip away — it's the same scene as the morning.
facilitatorNotes: Default ambient scene for the second push.
Reuses the stuck-recovery pattern from Build 1 and Build 2 first push.

blocks.title: Forty minutes left. Make it count.
blocks.body: Same discipline as the first push. The test is whether you can act on the signal from the intermezzo.

items.title: 14:50–15:20 · Iterate and extend.
items.body: Thirty minutes of building. Apply what the intermezzo surfaced. Push further than the first push did.

items.title: 15:20–15:30 · Final cleanup.
items.body: Ten minutes. Commit. Make your repo readable. Decide what you will show at Reveal.

blocks.title: Stuck? Return to the proof.
blocks.body: Same recovery pattern as the morning. State what you're trying to prove. Find the blind spot in the repo. Add the smallest check. Any executable signal beats more guessing.

phases.label: Závěr a reflexe
phases.goal: Uzavřít den přes konkrétní signály, které pomáhají práci přežít předání, a převést je do další praxe i do lepšího workshopového systému.
phases.roomSummary: Závěr a reflexe nejsou soutěž týmů. Sledujeme, které signály pomáhají práci přežít předání a které ji brzdí.
facilitatorPrompts: Nehodnotíme, který tým byl lepší.
Díváme se na systém: které signály pomáhají práci přežít předání a které ji brzdí.
Sbíráme konkrétní příklady, ne obecné dojmy.
watchFors: Reflexe sklouzne do hodnocení týmů.
Otázky zůstanou příliš obecné a bez vazby na repo.
Nevznikne další krok pro příští týden ani pro zlepšení blueprintu.
checkpointQuestions: Co vám pomohlo pokračovat?
Co chybělo?
Co uděláte jinak příští týden?

facilitatorRunner.goal: Uzavřít den přes konkrétní signály, které pomáhají práci přežít předání, a převést je do další praxe i do lepšího workshopového systému.
say: Tyhle postupy jsou nezávislé na nástroji. Když v pondělí budete pracovat s jiným toolchainem, principy platí: kontext v trasovaných artefaktech, plán před pohybem, ověření před důvěrou.
do: Po W3 spusťte pondělní závazky: každý napíše jednu větu. Seberte je.

scenes.label: Alone, pairs, fours, all
scenes.title: Alone, pairs, fours, all
scenes.body: Four layers of reflection. Each one a little wider than the last. This is the most important twenty minutes of the day — do not rush it.
facilitatorNotes: Full 1-2-4-All. Real time on every layer (3 + 4 + 5 + 10 min).

blocks.title: Alone, pairs, fours, all
blocks.body: Four layers of reflection. Each one a little wider than the last.

items.title: Alone · 3 minutes.
items.body: Individual writing. What signal helped you continue after rotation? What was missing? What's the one thing from today you actually want in your own work next time?

items.title: Pairs · 4 minutes.
items.body: Turn to one neighbor. Compare what you wrote. Find the one thing between the two of you that's most worth sharing.

items.title: Fours · 5 minutes.
items.body: Two pairs become a four. Compare again. Find the signal, the gap, or the insight that the four of you think the whole room needs to hear.

items.title: All · 10 minutes.
items.body: One thing per group of four, shared with the whole room. Brief. Specific. Honest. No summaries, no rankings.

blocks.title: This isn't about winners.
blocks.body: We're looking for the signals that helped work survive handoff and the ones that still break under pressure. Every repeated pain across teams is a candidate for a better template, check, or runbook — not a team failure.

scenes.label: Show us what you built
scenes.title: Show us what you built
scenes.body: Each team has five minutes. You just spent twenty minutes articulating what you learned — now show us what carries it. Run the tracer. Walk the repo. Say the thing that surprised you most.
facilitatorNotes: Five minutes per team, no Q&A during demos. Hold questions for after.
With 6+ teams, decide between 5 min each (runs long) or 4 min each (tightest).

blocks.title: Show us what you built
blocks.body: Five minutes per team. Share your screen. Run what runs. Walk the repo.

items.title: One team at a time, five minutes each.
items.body: Share your screen. Run what runs. Walk the repo. Point at something specific.

items.title: Three beats for each demo.
items.body: The running code or repo walk (most of the five minutes). The single signal you wished had been in this repo when you arrived. The single thing you're proud of leaving behind.

items.title: No Q&A during demos.
items.body: Hold questions for after. Each team gets their five minutes clean.

blocks.title: The reflection did the work.
blocks.body: You spent twenty minutes in the 1-2-4-All figuring out what you learned. The demo isn't about finding words — you already have them. It's about showing the thing the words point at.

scenes.label: What I saw today
scenes.title: What I saw today
scenes.body: Now my part. I walked the room all day. I read every team's card as the afternoon moved. Here's what I saw from outside — the patterns, the surprises, the things everyone collectively figured out that maybe nobody felt individually.
facilitatorNotes: This is the hardest beat in the day. Prepare by reading team cards during the pre-Reveal buffer, not by preparing generic remarks.
Uses the team-trail chrome preset so facilitator can point at specific team card entries as evidence.

blocks.title: What I saw today
blocks.body: Two or three patterns, one or two surprises, and the craft observation — what this group did well. Grounded, specific, honest. Not a pep talk.

blocks.title: Evidence, not praise.
blocks.body: The facilitator's observations should be grounded in what's on the team cards and what was just shared in the 1-2-4-All and the demos. Not general encouragement. Specific signals, specific surprises, specific patterns. If you can't point at it, don't say it.

scenes.label: The one thing you'll change
scenes.title: The one thing you'll change
scenes.body: One sentence. Specific enough that you could tell, a week from now, whether you actually did it. Write it first. Then pick where it lives.
facilitatorNotes: Four storage options. Participants pick one. No Monday framing — anchored to "the next time   work with an agent."

blocks.title: The one thing you'll change
blocks.body: One sentence. Specific enough that you could tell, a week from now, whether you actually did it.

items.title: Write the sentence (2 min).
items.body: Format: "The next time   work with an agent, the first thing I'll change is [specific action] — because [specific reason from today]." No vague language. No "I will try…..."

items.title: Pick where it lives (1 min).
items.body: Somewhere you'll actually see it the next time you start a session. Options in the callout below.

items.title: Say it out loud at your table (3 min).
items.body: One person at a time. No debate, no defense. Just read it.

blocks.title: Four options — pick one.
blocks.body: Pick whichever actually fits how you work: (1) The workshop skill on your machine — save the commitment with the skill's notes command; it lives with the skill you just practiced. (2) A physical card — we handed these out at the start of Reveal. (3) An email to yourself — send it now; it lands in your inbox tonight with today's context still fresh. (4) Wherever you already keep your own notes. Pick one. Do not pick none.

blocks.title: Specific enough to check.
blocks.body: Not: "I'll work better with agents." Yes: "The next tim  I start an agent task, I'll write Goal, Context, Constraints, and Done-When in AGENTS.md before typing anything into the prompt." If you can't te l a week from now whether you did it, the sentence isn't specific enough yet.

blocks.title: Tool-agnostic.
blocks.body: Everything you practiced today works with any coding agent — Codex, Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, whatever shows up next year. The repo matters. The verification matters. The handoff matters. The tool doesn't.

scenes.label: Go use it
scenes.title: Go use it
scenes.body: This is where my part ends and yours continues. You built something today that a stranger continued without you — that was the test, and you passed. The craft you practiced is yours. The materials stay with you.
facilitatorNotes: 30-60 seconds of screen time. The facilitator's closing words are more important than what's on the slide.
No "see you on Monday." No pep talk. The room feels more when the closing undersells.

blocks.title: Go use it
blocks.body: This is where my part ends and yours continues. You built something today that a stranger continued without you — that was the test, and you passed. The craft you practiced is yours. I won't be in the room next time you open an agent session — but everything you need is already in your hands.

blocks.title: The follow-up package.
blocks.body: The workshop skill and reference materials live with you after today. When you want to pull a pattern, a check, or a reminder, ask the skill. It knows what you learned because you were part of building it.

briefs.title: Code Review Helper
briefs.problem:
Code review bývá nevyrovnané. Některé změny projdou s dobrým checklistem a jasným popisem rizik, jiné bez společného rámce. Reviewer pak improvizuje, autor neví, co má ověřit předem, a tým ztrácí konzistenci právě tam, kde by měl být nejpřesnější.

Vaším úkolem je navrhnout nástroj, který z diffu nebo změny vytvoří použitelný review checklist a zároveň jasně oddělí jistotu, heuristiku a místa, kde je pořád potřeba lidský úsudek.
userStories: Jako reviewer chci z diffu rychle získat checklist změněných hranic, rizik, otázek a míst, na která se zaměřit.
Jako autor změny chci vědět, co mám ověřit ještě před samotným review.
Jako tým po rotaci chci navázat na heuristiky, které původní tým objevil, místo abych je znovu vymýšlel.
architectureNotes: Může jít o CLI, web nebo jednoduchý skript. Důležitý je jasný tok `diff → rubric → checklist`.
Musí být zřejmé, jaké vstupy nástroj očekává, co umí označit jistě a co naopak zůstává heuristické.
Přidejte seed diff nebo `examples/`, aby šlo workflow lokálně ověřit a další tým rychle přidal nové pravidlo.
Nástroj má pomáhat reviewerovi, ne předstírat neomylnost.
acceptanceCriteria: Nástroj vytvoří review checklist ze seed diffu.
Výstup odlišuje jistá zjištění od doporučení, hypotéz a bodů pro lidský úsudek.
Je jasné, jak přidat nové pravidlo nebo heuristiku bez dlouhého onboardingu.
Další tým může během 10 minut pokračovat v rozvoji bez chaosu.
briefs.firstAgentPrompt: Nezačínej kódem. Nejdřív napiš review rubric, tok vstupů a definici toho, co znamená dobrý checklist. Ukaž, kde je jistota, kde heuristika a co musí posoudit člověk. Teprve potom navrhni první implementační slice.

briefs.title: DevToolbox CLI
briefs.problem:
Ve skoro každém týmu vznikají malé jednorázové skripty: na čištění logů, převody JSONu, dohledání podezřelých commitů nebo rychlé kontroly nad repem. Fungují chvíli, často jen u jednoho člověka, a po pár dnech už nikdo neví, jak je spustit nebo rozšířit.

Vaším úkolem je navrhnout CLI nástroj, který řeší několik běžných developerských úloh tak, aby přežil handoff na jiný tým a nerozsypal se po přidání dalšího commandu.
userStories: Jako vývojář chci jedním příkazem převést log nebo JSON do čitelné podoby.
Jako vývojář chci rychle dohledat podezřelé commity, větve nebo změny bez ručního skládání git příkazů.
Jako tým chci mít příkazy i způsob práce popsané tak, aby po rotaci mohl bez zmatku pokračovat někdo jiný.
architectureNotes: Jazyk i framework si zvolte sami, ale CLI musí být snadno spustitelné a snadno objevitelné.
Od začátku oddělte samotné příkazy od pomocných utilit a konfigurace.
`AGENTS.md` má popsat build/test flow, konvence pro výstupy a pravidla pro další rozšiřování.
Stejně důležitý jako funkční příkaz je i runbook pro tým, který projekt převezme po obědě.
Nejde o pytel skriptů. Jde o malý systém, ve kterém je jasné, kde přibude další command, test a dokumentace.
acceptanceCriteria: Existují alespoň 3 užitečné příkazy nebo subcommands.
`README` i `AGENTS.md` popisují lokální spuštění a způsob ověření.
Je jasné, kde přidat další příkaz bez rozbití struktury projektu.
Nový tým zvládne během 10 minut přidat nebo opravit další command.
Každý command má aspoň jednu čitelnou ukázku vstupu a výstupu.
briefs.firstAgentPrompt: Nejdřív navrhni minimální architekturu, která přežije handoff. Začni `AGENTS.md`, flow pro přidání dalšího commandu a prvním ověřením. Teprve pak implementuj první command.

briefs.title: Doc Generator
briefs.problem:
Dokumentace stárne skoro okamžitě. Jakmile je její údržba čistě ruční, tým ji začne odkládat a po pár iteracích už nikdo neví, jestli popisuje realitu. Po handoffu pak nový tým neví, čemu má věřit a co je jen odhad.

Vaším úkolem je navrhnout nástroj, který z projektu vygeneruje základní technickou dokumentaci nebo strukturovaný přehled tak, aby bylo zřejmé, co nástroj opravdu ví a co si jen domýšlí.
userStories: Jako vývojář chci z projektu rychle získat základní technickou dokumentaci bez ručního sepisování všeho od nuly.
Jako reviewer chci během 5 minut pochopit strukturu modulů a hlavní vstupní body.
Jako tým po rotaci chci objevit architekturu projektu bez dlouhého pátrání po souvislostech.
architectureNotes: Vstup může být lokální repo, seed adresář nebo zjednodušený dataset.
Výstup může být Markdown, HTML nebo jednoduchý textový report.
Důležité je vysvětlit, co nástroj umí spolehlivě odvodit a co je jen heuristika.
Od začátku navrhněte strukturu tak, aby šlo časem přidat další typ výstupu.
Neřešte AI show. Řešte důvěryhodnost, dohledatelnost a další safe move pro tým po handoffu.
acceptanceCriteria: Nástroj vytvoří aspoň jednu čitelnou dokumentační stránku nebo report.
Je jasné, jak se nástroj spouští lokálně a nad jakým vstupem.
Výstup odděluje fakta od odhadů nebo heuristik.
Další tým umí přidat nový typ výstupu bez chaosu v repu.
Reviewer během 10 minut pozná, odkud které tvrzení pochází.
briefs.firstAgentPrompt: Nejdřív napiš, jaké signály budeš z projektu číst, které výstupy budou jistota a které jen heuristika. Teprve potom navrhni první implementační slice.

briefs.title: Metrics Dashboard
briefs.problem:
Týmy často data mají, ale chybí jim obrazovka, která z nich udělá rychle čitelný přehled. Bez toho se hůř rozhoduje, hůř diskutuje a každý si z čísel odnese něco jiného.

Vaším úkolem je navrhnout jednoduchý dashboard, který z několika metrik vytvoří srozumitelný společný pohled a zůstane čitelný i po handoffu na jiný tým.
userStories: Jako tým chci zobrazit několik metrik na jedné obrazovce tak, aby z nich šlo rychle vyčíst stav.
Jako facilitátor chci snadno měnit seed data bez zásahu do UI logiky.
Jako tým po rotaci chci během 10 minut pochopit strukturu dat, komponent i obrazovek.
architectureNotes: Seed data a UI oddělte hned od prvního commitu.
Mobile-first je výhoda, ale dashboard musí být dobře čitelný i na projekci.
README a monitoring mají vysvětlit, co už funguje, co je mock a co zatím chybí.
Myslete na to, aby přidání další metriky nevedlo k přepisování celé obrazovky.
Neoptimalizujte jen vzhled. Hlídejte, aby nový tým rychle pochopil datový model, layout pravidla a způsob ověření.
acceptanceCriteria: Dashboard ukáže alespoň 3 metriky a jeden trend nebo srovnání.
Repo popisuje datové zdroje i mock fallback.
Je jasné, kde se přidává nová metrika a jak se ověřuje layout.
Nový tým zvládne rozšířit dashboard bez rozbití struktury.
Layout je čitelný na mobilu i na větší obrazovce a je jasné, jak to ověřit.
briefs.firstAgentPrompt: Navrhni dashboard, který zvládne handoff. Nejdřív popiš datový model, komponenty, layout pravidla a kritéria `Hotovo když`, teprve potom stav UI.

briefs.title: Standup Bot
briefs.problem:
Denní standupy v chatu často končí jako dlouhé vlákno bez struktury. Blokery zapadnou, návaznosti mezi lidmi nejsou vidět a po pár hodinách už se těžko dohledává, co se vlastně domluvilo.

Vaším úkolem je navrhnout nástroj, který ze standup vstupů vytvoří přehled, se kterým se dá dál pracovat i bez původního autora nebo bez otevřeného původního vlákna.
userStories: Jako team lead chci sesbírat standup odpovědi do jednoho přehledného souhrnu.
Jako vývojář chci rychle vidět blokery, dependency a témata, která potřebují domluvu.
Jako tým po rotaci chci pochopit datový tok i integrační body bez ústního handoffu.
architectureNotes: Upřednostněte jasný datový model před složitou integrací.
Mock data jsou v pořádku, pokud workflow působí realisticky a je dobře popsané.
Oddělte ingest, zpracování a prezentaci výstupu.
Prompty, runbooky a rozhodnutí musí být uložené v repu, ne jen v hlavách původního týmu.
Neřešte „hezký summary text“ dřív než to, jestli jsou vidět blokery, dependency a další safe move.
acceptanceCriteria: Nástroj umí ingestovat seed data a vytvořit čitelný souhrn.
Výstup zvýrazní blokery nebo položky, které potřebují pozornost.
Repo obsahuje instrukce, jak řešení napojit na reálný chat nebo jiný vstupní kanál.
Po rotaci může nový tým pokračovat v práci z README a `AGENTS.md` bez ústního handoffu.
Je jasné, co je jisté shrnutí a co je jen heuristika nebo návrh.
briefs.firstAgentPrompt: Rozděl práci na ingest, sumarizaci a kontext pro další tým. Nejdřív napiš datový model, jistoty vs. heuristiky a dokumentaci, kterou nový tým otevře jako první. Až potom navrhni implementační kroky.

challenges.title: Vytvořte AGENTS.md jako mapu
challenges.description: Zapište cíl, build/test flow, trvalá pravidla a kam má další tým sáhnout jako první.

challenges.title: Napište skill pro code review
challenges.description: Alespoň jedna opakovatelná review rutina musí být v repu jako skill nebo runbook, ne jen v promptu.

challenges.title: Použijte /plan před kódováním
challenges.description: Nechte agenta nejprve rozplánovat práci, ukažte z čeho se vycházelo a dopište další safe move.

challenges.title: Přidejte nejmenší užitečné ověření
challenges.description: Vytvořte RED test, tracer bullet nebo jednoduchý browser check dřív, než dáte agentovi víc autonomie.

challenges.title: Spusťte 2 paralelní Codex sessions
challenges.description: Rozdělte problém do dvou nezávislých proudů a porovnejte, co se osvědčilo.

challenges.title: Přidejte 'Done When' ke každému tasku
challenges.description: Každý důležitý task musí mít explicitní kritérium dokončení a odkaz na ověření.
